features.summary: NVIDIA reported record Q3 fiscal 2021 revenue of $4.73 billion, up 57% year-over-year and 22% sequentially, driven by strong performance across gaming and data center segments. Gaming revenue hit a record $2.27 billion with the successful launch of RTX 30 series GPUs based on the new Ampere architecture, though supply constraints are limiting availability. Data center revenue reached a record $1.9 billion, up 162% year-over-year, powered by A100 GPU adoption and continued Mellanox networking growth. The company expects Q4 revenue of $4.8 billion despite some sequential decline in Mellanox due to non-recurring China OEM sales.
features.management_outlook: Management expressed strong confidence in long-term growth drivers, particularly AI inference which they view as their largest near-term opportunity. They expect gaming to continue growing sequentially in Q4 despite typical seasonality, driven by RTX 30 series ramp. The data center business is expected to see slight sequential decline due to Mellanox timing but underlying compute products should grow mid-single digits. Management highlighted early-stage opportunities in DPUs, Omniverse collaboration platform, and the pending Arm acquisition to create a comprehensive AI computing company.
features.risks: Primary risks include industry-wide supply chain constraints and long cycle times that may take several months to resolve, limiting product availability despite strong demand. The company faces potential competition from cloud customers developing internal chips, though management views this as validation of the acceleration trend. Mellanox revenue will see meaningful sequential decline in Q4 due to non-recurring China OEM sales, and there's uncertainty around enterprise recovery timing given work-from-home impacts on technology deployments.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers include the RTX 30 series gaming GPU launch creating the best upgrade opportunity in a decade for 200+ million GeForce users, accelerating AI inference adoption across cloud and enterprise customers, and A100 GPU ramp across all major cloud providers. The transition to software-defined disaggregated data center architectures is driving Mellanox networking demand, while emerging opportunities in DPUs, autonomous vehicles, and edge computing represent significant long-term growth vectors. The pending Arm acquisition positions NVIDIA to extend from cloud to trillions of edge devices.
